markerText: Quanah Parker spoke downtown, 1909 Matador Ranch hosted chief & wives other Comanches camped N of jail
mainImageAlt: Photo of the Matador arrow in Motley County. Taken by Barbara Brannon.

galleryImages.alt: Photo of the Matador arrow in Motley County
Photo of the Matador arrow in Motley County
Photo of the Matador arrow in Motley County

geography_md:
Motley County sits at a dramatic geological crossroads that was vital to the Comanches: the rugged breaks where the flat High Plains of the Llano Estacado fracture and descend into the Rolling Plains. For Quanah Parker and the Kwahadi band, this geography was not just scenery; it was strategic infrastructure.

The Caprock Escarpment, visible to the west, provided a formidable natural fortress, while the nutrient-rich grasslands below supported the massive bison herds that were the lifeblood of the tribe. The region is dissected by the tributaries of the Pease River, most notably the Middle Pease, which carved out shelter canyons and reliable water sources essential for nomadic encampments.

Matador lies in the heart of this transition zone. Unlike the exposed high plains, Matador’s location in the Rolling Plains offered timber and protection from fierce winter winds, making it a favored wintering ground. Nearby Roaring Springs served as a critical oasis waypoint; its permanent fresh water and natural protection made it a documented campsite for Quanah Parker. Even after the reservation era, the geography drew Quanah back—he frequently traveled this familiar corridor to visit rancher friends, bridging his past as a warrior of the Breaks with his later life as a statesman.

Today, the Quanah Parker Trail arrow in Matador (located at the historic jail) marks this landscape not just as a settlement, but as a favored corridor of the Comanche.
installation_details_md:
The Matador arrow was installed in Matador on August 10, 2011. Charles Smith and community volunteers installed the arrow on Wednesday afternoon, August 10, in record heat. While the sun was blazing, the 21-foot arrow found its permanent home south of Pioneer Park, thanks to County Judge Jim Meador, who donated the location to mark the Quanah Parker Trail in Matador.

**--Matador Tribune, August 18, 2011.**

On Saturday, October 15, 2011, the Giant Arrow Dedication took place at Pioneer Park in downtown Matador. More than 100 guests attended the event, which was sponsored by the Friends of the Historic Motley County Jail. David Stafford provided barbecue brisket, chips, and drinks. Seating consisted of lawn chairs brought by guests and overturned flower pots. The staging area included floral decorations and a sound system donated by the Chamber of Commerce and operated by Seab and Dianne Washington. The jail board conducted a raffle for a luggage set, which was won by Adrianne Hamilton.

On Saturday, October 15, 2011, the Giant Arrow Dedication took place at Pioneer Park in downtown Matador. More than 100 guests attended the event, which was sponsored by the Friends of the Historic Motley County Jail. David Stafford provided barbecue brisket, chips, and drinks. Seating consisted of lawn chairs brought by guests and overturned flower pots. The staging area included floral decorations and a sound system donated by the Chamber of Commerce and operated by Seab and Dianne Washington. The jail board conducted a raffle for a luggage set, which was won by Adrianne Hamilton.

County Judge Jim Meador delivered a welcome address. Deborah Sue McDonald, Executive Director of the Texas Plains Trail Region (TPTR), spoke on heritage tourism and noted a recent partnership grant for the Motley County Media Project. McDonald introduced Marisue Potts, Chair of the Motley County Historical Commission, who discussed the county's historical connection to Quanah Parker. Dr. Holle Humphries also spoke regarding the cultural significance of the trail. The Giant Arrow sculpture, donated by Charles Smith of New Home, Texas, marks the first installation designating historical Comanche sites along the trail.

Following the speeches, Annalon Gilbreath of Midland, Texas, performed a historical re-enactment as Mary Ann Goodnight. Her performance included a song on the autoharp and an unaccompanied vocal performance.

The event concluded with a presentation by the Parker family. Anna Tahmahkera, 93, sang and prayed in Comanche while displaying a traditional buckskin dress and moccasins. Carolyn Wilson introduced Ardith Parker Leming, Quanah Parker’s great-granddaughter. Her husband, Glen Leming, demonstrated the traditional tanning process for buffalo hides and displayed bows and arrows. Paul Davis read a poem titled 'The Spirit of Quanah Parker Lives On.' Glen Leming and Paul Davis then conducted a cedar blessing ceremony near the Giant Arrow and the American, Texas, and Comanche flags, officially dedicating the site.

**--Matador Tribune, August 15, 2011.**

markerText: None
history_md:
On March 15, 2013, Charles A. Smith of New Home, Texas hauled in by pick-up truck and trailer, his work of public art contributed to Levelland, Texas. At the invitation of Levelland city officials, and under the historical guidance provided by John and Bette Hope with fellow members of the Hockley County Historical Commission, and with the mechanical prowess of city workers and muscle power contributed by volunteers of the Texas Plains Trail Region and Levelland citizens -- all helped to hoist the 21-foot welded steel Quanah Parker Arrow up into the air to stand as a sentinel overlooking Lobo Lake.

Decorated with bands of red, yellow and blue to reflect the colors seen on the shield of the Comanche Nation, the Arrow that stands in the grund at this site commemorates an important historical time period and people, and event that took place nearby.

From 1780 through 1875, the lands of Hockley County were once included within the Comanchería, the territorial range of the Comanches who once dominated the West and North Central area of what is today known as 'Texas.'

And as the lands of Hockley County figure hugely in Comanche history, it is only fitting that a Quanah Parker Trail Arrow came to be installed in Levelland, the county seat.

The Yellow House Draw which once served as a major water trailway followed by game animals and those who hunted them, bisects Hockley County diagonally, conducting waters that flowed from the NW to the SE. This water trailway served as a major trade route. It connected the commerce conducted between the settled village Pueblo people who raised crops in New Mexico to the west, with the nomadic Comanches who hunted bison on the Llano Estacado and central Texas plains, to the east.

But as European explorers began to advance on their frontier beginning in the 16th century, conflict arose as to who had claim on the land. The Comancbes, along with allied warriors of other Southern Plains tribes, joined forces to push back. But by 1875, the U.S. military succeeded in coercing nearly all Native Americans onto reservations.

Yet, all did not accept their fate with resignation. By 1876, inept administration committed by the U.S. government, which was supposed to honor the terms of the Medicine Lodge Treaty of 1867, and feed all those Indians who had agreed to come into the reservations -- brought the inhabitants of the Kiowa Comanche Apache reservation at Fort Sill in Indian Territory (Oklahoma), to the brink of starvation.

In December of 1876, a contingent of Comanches led by Red Young Man, rode off the reservation, to return to the plains of Texas in search of game with which to feed themselves -- particularly -- the once plentiful bison they had depended upon for food. Finding that the bison had been slaughtered to near extinction by buffalo hunters, still at work on the Texas plains, the Indians launched attack on the buffalo hunters and raided their camps. One buffalo hunter was killed.

Back at the reservation, Quanah Parker, who by 1877 was regarded among U.S. Army officials and Indian agents as a leader among the Comanche people, requested permission to bring the Comanche insurgents back to Fort Sill. Knowing the unfortunate circumstances of near-starvation that had driven them to their actions in the first place, Col. Ranald S. Mackenzie, commander of Fort Sill, gave Quanah a letter to take with him, granting him the authority to act in this capacity.

In July 1877, out on the Llano Estacado plains of Texas, Capt. Nicholas Nolan, commanding officer of the Tenth Cavalry, Troop A, along with a group of buffalo hunters, began their quest to locate and capture the Comanches at large. Instead, Nolan was thwarted by Quanah Parker. Quanah, having arrived at Nolan's camp by July 18 and showing him the letter, avoided revealing where he thought the Comanches were located, by evasively communicating through a translator, former Comanchero trader José Tafoya. Essentially, Quanah pointed Nolan toward the wrong direction. Quanah, familiar with the obscure locations of scarce water sources largely unknown to the U.S. Army, was able to track down and find the Comanche contingent, and lead them far away from encountering Nolan and his men. Nolan, impetuously, had sent his men to the northwest, to give chase to the Indians in a direction that he thought the Indians had headed for -- forgetting to order his soldiers to fill their canteens with water before departing camp and embarking out upon the drought-stricken West Texas plains. Once realizing the futility of their quest, and struggling with thirst, the soliders doubled back, heading back to their base camp to the southeast, located near to the eastern edge of the Llano Estacado. This return path took them through the central lands of present-day Hockley County, past present-day Levelland, a route where there were no springs to provide sources of water. As a consquence, four of Nolan's soldiers died horribly of thirst.

But Quanah safely conducted the Comanches back to Ft. Sill and the reservation.

This historical episode that played out across the lands of Hockley and its neighboring counties, is recounted with fascinating detail, by Texas Tech Professor Emeritus of History, Paul H. Carlson, in The Buffalo Solider Tragedy of 1877, published 2003 by Texas A&M Press of College Station.

The Quanah Parker Trail Arrow installed at the site of Lobo Lake in Levelland of Hockley County stands as a symbol to help today's travelers who come through the area learn, that before we all came here, a nomadic tribe of indigenous people regarded this area their home. Such was their prowess at finding water, hunting game and holding invaders at bay until the late 19th century, that this land was last regarded as their territory.

And so it was that the lands in the location of Levelland in Hockley County -- were once embraced within -- the Comanchería.

In recognition of that history, on August 26, 2016, members of the Comanche Nation and direct descendants of Quanah Parker journeyed as honored guests to Levelland by invitation of the Hockley County Historical Commission. In the company of Levelland's citizens and school children, they conducted a Cedar smoke ceremony to confer their Comanche blessing on the Arrow.
visitor_info_md: This arrow is located at Lobo Lake Park in Levelland. There is parking to the north of the arrow.

arrowName: Charles and Mary Ann Goodnight Ranch State Historic Site
location: Located at the north edge of the parking lot near the historic house at the Charles and Mary Ann Goodnight Ranch State Historic Site.
markerText: The legacy of Quanah Parker and Charles Goodnight is that former enemies can become good friends
geography_md:
The geographical setting of the area near the historical marker, rooted in Charles Goodnight's original 1876 "Old Home Ranch," is defined by the dramatic and rugged landscape of Palo Duro Canyon. The name "Palo Duro" translates from Spanish to "hard wood or cedar," which perfectly describes the deep barrancas (ravines) and sheltered valleys that characterize the region. Early Spanish explorers noted that the canyon spanned a league from bank to bank, featuring a small river at the bottom and valleys lush with trees, wild grapes, mulberries, rose bushes, and plum thickets.

A central geographical feature of Goodnight's early ranch site was its vital hydrology, specifically the presence of "Home Springs." Located in a remote area roughly six kilometers north of present-day Wayside, these fresh-water springs were the lifeblood of the 1876 headquarters. The water was so cool and reliable that it originally flowed directly through Goodnight's meat and milk cooling house, though environmental changes mean the springs now typically only flow rarely or during the wet months. Historically, the canyon's hydrology was incredibly robust; as late as 1928, nearly all tributaries in the canyon boasted year-round flowing springs and numerous waterfalls.

This rich, spring-fed environment created a thriving oasis that supported diverse wildlife and human habitation for centuries. The areas around the canyon's springs were lush with water-loving plants like cottonwoods, willows, salt cedars, cattails, and rushes.^4 This dense vegetation provided an ideal habitat for an immense variety of animals, including bears, buffalo, mule deer, wolves, panthers, elk, and wild turkeys. Because of this natural abundance and the crucial shelter provided by the canyon walls during bitter cold winters with heavy snow, the area served as a favorite winter campground for the Comanches and other Native American tribes long before ranchers arrived.
history_md:
The presence of a Quanah Parker Trail arrow at the Charles and Mary Ann Goodnight Ranch State Historic Site commemorates a pivotal, peaceful encounter in 1878 between two monumental figures of the Texas Panhandle: Quanah Parker and rancher Charles Goodnight. In the bitter cold of early 1878, Quanah Parker and his followers temporarily left their reservation and returned to their traditional camping grounds in Palo Duro Canyon. At the time, Goodnight was operating out of his "Old Home Ranch" headquarters in the upper canyon. When Goodnight learned the Indians were killing his cattle for survival, he bravely rode out to meet them, believing that a direct approach was the safest way to prevent a violent battle.

The arrow's significance is deeply rooted in the historic treaty negotiated during a tense parley between Goodnight and the Comanche leaders at the ranch. Surrounded by warriors for an interrogation, Goodnight successfully convinced them he was from Colorado rather than a "Tejano," knowing their deep bitterness toward Texans. This diplomatic maneuvering led to the famous treaty between Quanah Parker and Goodnight. Goodnight promised to provide the Indians with two beeves every other day until they could find buffalo herds, in exchange for Quanah's guarantee to maintain order and protect the ranch's property.

Ultimately, the marker commemorates an extraordinary bond of mutual respect that transcended the cultural conflicts of the era. The Native Americans gained immense reverence for Goodnight, dubbing him the "Dangerous Man" and the "Leopard Coat Man" after witnessing his incredible marksmanship when he flawlessly shot twenty-five cattle on the run to provision their return trip to Fort Sill. In turn, Goodnight held deep admiration for Quanah, publicly stating that he never knew the Chief to break a promise or a treaty, and noted his honorable conduct in battle. This unique foundation of trust between these two leaders was crucial to the early success and peace of the Panhandle region.
visitor_info_md:
Visitors may park in the parking lot at the historic house past the visitors center. Gate may be closed during times that the historic site is not open.
* [Charles and Mary Ann Goodnight Ranch State Historic Site](https://thc.texas.gov/historic-sites/goodnight-ranch) | 2000 US-287 | Clarendon, Texas 79226 | (806) 944-5591

* [Armstrong County Museum](https://www.armstrongcountymuseum.net/) | 120 Trice Street | Claude, Texas  79019 | (806) 226-2187
bibliography_md:
1. Burton, Harley True. "History of the JA Ranch" (Master's Thesis, University of Texas at Austin, June 1927). Incorporating interviews conducted with Charles Goodnight in 1926 and 1927.
2. Goodnight, Charles. "True Sketch Of Quanah Parker's Life," *Frontier Times Magazine*, Vol. 4 No. 2, November 1926.
3. Gunnar Brune, *Springs of Texas*. Fort Worth: Branch-Smith, 1981.

installation_details_md: "Before wagon roads, railroads and highways, travelers followed waterways like Coldwater Creek across the Llano Estacado. Even in the post-reservation era, observers reported seeing Indians crossing east-west -- first to the west on poor horses with sorry tack and then back to the east with better horses and nicer saddles. No doubt, the Comanche were among these tribes who traveled across present-day Sherman County, certainly long before it was a county and also after Anglo-European settlers moved into the area. For the Comanche, horses -- brought to North America by the Spaniards -- were their stock in trade. Comanche trade routes extended across present-day Texas and Oklahoma north and west into Colorado and New Mexico and south to Mexico." --Previous QPT Website

location: XIT General Ranch Office in Channing
markerText: None
geography_md:
Situated in the southeastern corner of Hartley County in the northwestern Texas Panhandle, the landscape surrounding Channing sits at a high, wind-swept elevation of roughly 3,800 feet above sea level. This region defines the physical transition from the immense, dead-flat expanse of the Llano Estacado into the rolling, semiarid High Plains. Here, deep clay and sandy loam soils feed dense native shortgrass prairies that have historically anchored massive cattle ranching empires, most notably the legendary XIT Ranch. While the upland plains immediately surrounding the quiet county seat appear smoothly undulating, the geography is dynamically carved by a vital network of historical waterways, chiefly Rita Blanca Creek and Punta de Agua Creek.

These two primary drainages dictate the local topography, transforming the flat tableland into a rugged labyrinth of breaks, draws, and sharp arroyos as they cut paths toward the nearby Canadian River basin. Punta de Agua Creek (historically documented by early explorers as Major Long’s Creek) originates across the state line in New Mexico and flows southeastward across Hartley County. Passing just to the west and south of Channing, its broad, ordinarily dry sandy bed is lined with resilient native grasses, water-tolerant hardwoods, and occasional conifers. Though it remains dry for much of the year under the region's sparse eighteen inches of annual rainfall, Punta de Agua serves as a powerful conduit for sudden, heavy flash floods following torrential High Plains thunderstorms.

Just east of Channing, Punta de Agua converges with Rita Blanca Creek, a major regional waterway that flows southward through the eastern half of the county. Rita Blanca further scars the prairie landscape, carving out a pronounced valley that breaks the monotony of the surrounding plains. Together, these seasonal creek systems carry the localized runoff of the northern Panhandle into the Canadian River breaks just over the Oldham County line. The presence of these creeks is precisely why this specific pocket of Hartley County feels distinct from the unyielding flatness further south; they introduce a rugged, rolling relief of dramatic draws, seasonal pools, and rough breaks to an otherwise infinite horizon of grass and wind.
history_md:
Before it became the domain of the cattle baron, the rugged plains and deep draws surrounding Channing sat at the geographic heart of Comancheria, the vast, fiercely defended empire of the Comanche. While the high, windswept grasslands of present-day Hartley County offered prime hunting grounds, the true strategic value of this specific landscape lay in its hydrology. Long before the XIT Ranch fenced the open range, the deep valleys of Rita Blanca and Punta de Agua creeks served as vital, life-sustaining corridors in an otherwise arid expanse.

For the Comanche and other Plains tribes, Rita Blanca Creek was far more than a seasonal water source; it was a natural highway through the Llano Estacado. When raiding parties moved north to strike the heavily traveled Santa Fe Trail, which cut across the nearby Oklahoma Panhandle, or when they retreated south with captured horses and goods, Rita Blanca Creek provided a protected, easily navigable route. Its braided channels and cutbank draws offered reliable water, forage for horses, and natural concealment from the open plains. This deep indigenous history remains etched into the geography around Channing, where the very same creek breaks that later sheltered pioneer cattle herds once acted as the hidden staging grounds and thoroughfares for the master horsemen of the Southern Plains.

geography_md:
Armstrong County, located in the south-central Texas Panhandle, is defined by a dramatic and visually arresting geographical dichotomy: the stark, level expanse of the High Plains and the rugged, eroded breaks of the Caprock Escarpment. The northern and western portions of the county sit atop the Llano Estacado, a vast, remarkably flat plateau that elevates the region to approximately 3,400 feet above sea level. This area is characterized by deep, arable clay loam soils supporting extensive winter wheat and sorghum farming, typical of the wider Panhandle's agricultural landscape. However, this flatness is abruptly interrupted by the Caprock, a geological 'break' that slashes across the county, marking the transition from the high tableland to the lower rolling plains. This escarpment is not merely a slope but a distinct cliff-like feature that exposes millions of years of geological strata, serving as the defining boundary between the high plains and the chaotic topography below.

Dominating the southern and central geography is the spectacular incision of the Palo Duro Canyon, carved by the Prairie Dog Town Fork of the Red River. As the river winds through the county, it creates a labyrinth of colorful sandstone mesas, steep cliffs, and deep ravines that drop steeply from the high plains—sometimes varying in elevation by nearly 1,000 feet within a short distance. This 'Rough Broken Land,' as it is often classified in soil surveys, supports a distinct ecosystem of juniper, mesquite, and scrub oak, contrasting sharply with the shortgrass prairie above. The Mulberry Creek drainage also dissects the eastern part of the county, further contributing to the jagged, erratic terrain that historically made traversing this section of the Panhandle prevalent with natural barriers yet rich in shelter and water sources.
visitor_info_md: Located east of the Wayside Community Church.

installation_details_md: "The sun shone bright on August 29, 2014 for the installation of Castro County's Trail Arrow. The Easter and Dimmitt Lions Clubs helped Dimmitt acquire the arrow, installed August 29, 2014 and dedicated September 6, 2014, to recognize the presence of the Comanche and other Plains Indians in the area before the arrival of settlers. The discovery of artifacts has confirmed the presence of native peoples in the region. Also, the Comanchero Trail followed Running Water Draw."--Original QPT website

location: Located in Triangle Park west of Hansford County courthouse on Kenneth Avenue.
markerText: In Palo Duro Creek Canyon 6 miles W of Spearman, Comanches and Indians before them camped at historic springs
geography_md:
**Map Resources**
>1. [USGS Topographic Map of Hansford County](/img/arrows/81/arrow81map-1.png)
history_md:
The Quanah Parker Trail arrow in Triangle city park of Spearman, TX, installed March 17, 2015, stands as a reminder that long ago, Comanches and those before them camped at a historic springs six miles to the west, located in the canyon of Palo Duro Creek.  This fresh water source later attracted pioneers to settle near the site, and so they founded the first city of the county nearby, calling it Hansford, Texas.

Multiple springs as these once replenished Palo Duro Creek, which as far back as Prehistoric times, served as a major water trail way followed by bison and First Americans. Its North [Palo Duro Creek] and South [Palo Duro Creek] tributaries conjoin near the southern boundary of present-day Hansford County, to flow to the northeast.  The hooves of thousands of bison and leather moccasins of scores of early nomadic hunters followed the main pathway that the Palo Duro Creek carved into canyonlands from SW to the NE, supplying fresh water all along the way, to where it emptied into the North Canadian River in the present-day Panhandle of Oklahoma.  This hoof and foot traffic carved a preliminary pathway for what later would become the middle portion of the Old Tascosa-Dodge City Trail.  There is evidence that this traffic in bison and the nomadic hunters who tracked them along this major water trail way continued through time, dating as far back as 2000 years before the present, long before the Comanches arrived to this area in the late 18th century.  A burial for an Indian who preceded them was found sequestered along Hanna's Draw -- once called "Indian Draw" -- a tributary of Palo Duro Creek located to the north of present-day Morse.  Excavation undertaken to create the Palo Duro Creek Reservoir unearthed caches of lithic stone tools, including Alibates flint, and remnants of bison processing found along the western banks of Palo Duro Creek to the northeast.  The nearby discovery of the remnant relics of a fire pit estimated to be more than 500 years old can be seen today on exhibit at the Stationmaster's House Museum in Spearman.

What drew nomadic hunters to this area?  In the winter, when crops cannot be grown, and there is little plant life to be foraged, the bison that could be seen in the thousands to gather on the warmer Texas Southern Plains to graze and seek shelter from winds in its creek and river-carved canyonlands, proved to be an irresistible attraction.  In the winter, bison coats are at their shaggiest, and will produce the greatest warmth as robes for people chilled by the winter cold. With the bison gathering in the thousands in such places during the fall, hunters of entire tribes traveled along water trail ways such as Palo Duro Creek to make winter camps on the open Southern Plains  -- to include here, in Hansford County.  And after 1790, such tribes included the Comanches.(1)

The resource that drew Comanches to this area proved to be an irresistible magnet as well to those who came to be one of their greatest foes: white buffalo hunters.  

It was here, in Hansford County, in 1873, that the beginning of the end came for Quanah Parker and the force of allied Southern Plains warriors he would command without success on the following June of 1874 at the Battle of Adobe Walls in nearby Hutchinson County to turn the tide against the buffalo hunters -- when two sets of brothers, all determined to make their fortune slaughtering bison for profit, arrived to set up their buffalo hunting camps, along Palo Duro Creek.

The Medicine Lodge Treaty of 1867, brokered with many of the Southern Plains tribes in Kansas, was understood by Indians to reserve the Panhandle of Texas as a bison hunting ground exclusively for them -- and only for them -- to hunt the bison.  

However, by 1872, white buffalo hunters had so decimated bison herds in the Great Plains states to the north of Texas, that the hunters became anxious to push south of the Arkansas River -- and into Texas.  

In 1873, buffalo hunters Josiah Wright Mooar and Steel Frazier directly questioned the commander of Fort Dodge, Kansas, Major Richard Irving Dodge, whether they could hunt in Texas.(2)

"Major," asked Mooar, "If we cross into Texas, what will be the government's attitude toward us?  "Boys, he replied, if I were a buffalo hunter, I would hunt where the buffalos are."(3)

"Late in the summer, after hearing of large herds in the Texas Panhandle, Wright Mooar and John Webb rode south to see for themselves...Before they reached the breaks of the South Canadian, they found buffaloes in an almost solid mass as far as they could see. There were hundreds of thousands of them, fattening on the upland grass...After camping that night in the middle of the herd, they turned back north"....[eventually reaching the Cimarron and their home camp].(4)

And so it was that in the late fall of 1873, John Wesley and Josiah Wright Mooar, made their way south, into the Panhandle of Texas, where the bison herds in the thousands were gathering for winter.  Following the water trail way of Palo Duro Creek from NE to SW, into present-day Hansford County, Texas, they set up a buffalo hunting camp along Palo Duro Creek.

The Cator brothers, James H. and Bob Cator, also seeking their fortune as buffalo hunters, followed the lead of the Mooar's.  

"Late in the fall [of 1873] they followed Josiah Wright Mooar and John Wesley Mooar from Clay Center, Kansas, to the Texas Panhandle, where the animals were still abundant. A severe snowstorm on Christmas Day 1873 caught the Cators' hunting party in a break along North Palo Duro Creek (in what is now Hansford County) huddling against an earthen wall. There they constructed a crude dugout of cottonwood pickets and buffalo hides and waited out the winter."(5)

The Cator brothers had established their up their buffalo camp on Near to Nature Springs, a source of water for Palo Duro Creek.(6)

In this respect, they "were among the very first hunters to locate at the headwaters of the North Palo Duro Creek.  The site had good wood, water, and protection from the weather, but [also] it was in the center of the buffalo range.  They made a dugout in the side of a little slope and built a corral of cottonwood poles for their horses…[T]he Cators used this camp as the hub of their buffalo business enterprises for the next three years."(7)

The Cators and Mooars experienced success in their location, leading them to establish a trading center nearby that they called "Adobe Walls," after an earlier Bent trading post by the same name.  Their decision placed them in a prime spot in time for disaster: that followed on June 27, 1874...when those asleep in their trading post came into direct explosive contact with Quanah Parker and his force of allied South Plains Indian warriors...but that's a story to be told on behalf of Hutchinson County...
installation_details_md: "Present-day Hansford County, crisscrossed by highways, was once crossed by bison and Indian paths. The Comanche, Kiowa and Kiowa Apache hunted in the area and traded with Comancheros who drove their carteras along the ancient trails." -- Previous QPT Website
visitor_info_md:
The Quanah Parker Trail arrow in Spearman is located in Triangle Park on West Kenneth Avenue. Although there is no dedicated parking, it is possible to park on Andrews Street or Hancock Street.
#### Nearby Points of Interest:

* [J. B. Buchanan Windmill Park](https://www.facebook.com/pages/J-B-Buchanan-Windmill-Park/1567129860265127) | Hwy 207 South | Spearman, TX 79081
* [Lake Palo Duro](https://lakepaloduro.com/) | 14320 River Authority Road | Spearman, Texas 79081 | (806) 882-4401

location: Located on the southeast corner of the intersection at Main Street and 1st Street.
markerText: 19th c. Comanches & earlier Indians hunted along Yellow House Draw watered by streams near Anton
geography_md:
Anton is located in Hockley County, Texas, situated at a point where three tributaries historically converged to feed water into the Yellow House Draw. Key geographic features of the area include Yellow House Draw. The Yellow House Draw and the associated major water trail is a remnant of an ancient river that originally flowed from the northwest to the southeast across the Llano Estacado. It drains water off the Caprock into the canyonlands below to help form the Brazos River system. The draw cuts a gap of over thirty-five miles into the eastern edge of the Caprock escarpment. The river's headwaters rise in southeast Bailey County, specifically forming at the lakes of the Muleshoe National Wildlife Refuge and the Yellow House Ranch (which straddles Hockley and Lamb Counties). The watercourse likely expanded further northwest into New Mexico in the distant past. The canyon gets its name from Casas Amarillas (Yellow Houses) in Hockley and Lamb counties, where the draw curves east to expose a yellow cliff that resembles a distant city. At the dawn of history, the tributaries near Anton were fed by springs and seeps. However, these water sources evidently dried up around 1920 after the first wells were dug in the area, and the entirety of the draw is cultivated today. Despite this, if you look northwest across US 84 from Anton, you can still see a line of trees whose roots once sank into the damp soil, marking the historical edges of the draw.

**Map Resources**
>1. [Ancient Pathways, Lubbock Lake National Historic Landmark](/img/arrows/84/arrow84map-1.jpg)
>2. [1957 USGS Topographical Map showing three tributaries converging near Anton to contribute water to Yellow House Draw.](/img/arrows/84/arrow84map-2.jpg)
>3. [Map showing sources of Yellow House Draw at Casas Amarillas and Muleshoe Wildlife Refuge](/img/arrows/84/arrow84map-3.jpg)
history_md: Because it was a rare and vital source of water, the Yellow House Draw served as a major migration corridor and hunting ground for centuries. The draw functioned as a major 'water trail way' for wild game, such as bison and antelope, migrating in search of fresh grass. Consequently, nomadic hunters ranging from early Paleo-Indians to later Native American tribes followed this route to track the animals. The area's deep prehistoric roots were confirmed by the 1985 discovery of 'Ryan's cache' (named after the property owner). Located about thirteen miles southeast of Anton and just two and a half miles south of the draw, this collection of Plainview-type lithic artifacts was buried by a prehistoric hunter for safekeeping, with the intention of returning to refine the stones into spear points for hunting. Hundreds of years after the Paleo-Indians, Comanche tribes continued to utilize the site. They followed the wild game along the Yellow House Draw and are known to have convened at the main headwaters to the west, near today's Yellow House Ranch and Muleshoe National Wildlife Refuge.
installation_details_md: "A Quanah Parker Trail Arrow arrived Friday, May 6, 2016 in Anton for installation in Triangle City Park just across the railroad tracks from U. S. 84. Near Anton is Yellow House Draw, an old Comanche trail route. The images on this page show both the installation and, interspersed in the sequence, scenes in and around Anton."
visitor_info_md: There is no dedicated parking area, but the visitors can park on the side of the street to view the arrow.

-Charles G. Davis, "Yellow House Draw," *Handbook of Texas Online*, accessed May 18, 2026, https://www.tshaonline.org/handbook/entries/yellow-house-draw.
